title: Chapter 16: The Double Agent
content: Sandalwood hits me first.

I'm standing in the second-floor hallway of Soho House, the one with the Moroccan tiles and the recessed lighting that makes everyone look like they're posing for a magazine they'll never admit to reading. The incense drifts from some hidden receptor, thick enough to coat the back of my throat, sweet enough to make you forget that every conversation in this building is a transaction.

I'm here because Cassandra told me Jordan Keeler was hosting a "small dinner" tonight. Twelve people, maximum. The kind of event that doesn't get announced — it just happens, and if you're not at the table, you don't exist. I wasn't invited. I came anyway, hoping to grab Jordan after for a quote about the upcoming Vance Media restructure. Simple. Clean. Professional.
I'm not expecting what I find.

The private room at the end of the hall — the one they call the Grenfell Room, after the artist, not the tower — has its door ajar. And through that gap, I see Jordan Keeler emerge. They're wearing that cream linen blazer they love, the one with the mother-of-pearl buttons, and their expression is something I've never seen on Jordan's face before: blank. Not performative blankness, not the studied neutrality they deploy at parties. Just empty. Like someone wiped a hard drive.
And then a man steps out behind them.

Silver hair, swept back. Charcoal suit that costs more than my camera. A face like a closed fist — everything tight, nothing given away. He's older, maybe sixty, and he moves with the particular confidence of someone who has never once doubted that a room belongs to him.

I know him. Not personally. I know him from the photographs Tom Hargrove keeps on his office wall — the ones from the charity galas, the ones where Tom has his arm around the shoulders of men who matter. This is Whitfield. Tom's lawyer. The fixer. The man who makes problems disappear into settlement agreements and NDAs.

Jordan turns to him, and the exchange is so casual it could be choreographed. Jordan reaches into their blazer — left interior pocket — and pulls out a manila envelope. The paper is soft, well-handled, the corners already dog-eared. They hand it to Whitfield the way you'd pass someone the bread basket at dinner. No hesitation. No drama. Just a thing moving from one hand to another.

But my camera is already up. I don't even remember raising it. Muscle memory, the reflex of a documentarian who has learned that the most important moments are the ones nobody wants filmed. I snap two frames through the gap in the door, the shutter sound muffled by my palm.
In the second frame, the label on the envelope catches the light.
Two words. *VANCE — PERSONAL.*
My stomach drops like a cut elevator cable.

I wait on the street. November in Manhattan, the kind of cold that finds the gaps between your coat buttons and settles in. The heat grates exhale that particular New York smell — iron and old grease and something organic I've never been able to name. I lean against the bike rack across from Soho House's entrance, my fingers going numb inside my pockets, and I wait.

Jordan comes out eleven minutes later. They walk east, not west toward the subway, and they're holding a matcha latte in a green paper cup. Steam rises from the sip hole, curling into the dark air before dissolving. I fall into step beside them.
"Maya." Jordan doesn't sound surprised. That's the worst part. They sound like they've been waiting for this. Like they knew the bill would come due eventually.
"You want to tell me what that was?"

Jordan takes a sip of the matcha. Their eyes stay flat, but their hand — the one holding the cup — trembles. A small thing. Microscopic. The kind of tremor you'd miss if you weren't looking for it, the kind that says the body knows something the mouth won't admit.
"I work for everyone, Maya." They say it simply, like explaining water. "That's what connectors do. We move between worlds. That's the whole point."
"You're selling Jax out."

The words land harder than I expect. Jordan stops walking. A taxi blares past us, yellow and loud, and for a second the city intercedes and we're just two people on a sidewalk, nothing dramatic, nothing that matters.
Then Jordan turns to face me, and their expression is — complicated. Not defensive. Not guilty. Something closer to exhaustion.
"I'm selling information." Their voice is precise. "What people do with it is their business. I'm not a judge, Maya. I'm a channel."

"A channel." I taste the word and it's bitter. "That's what you're calling it? You handed a manila envelope labeled *Vance — Personal* to Tom Hargrove's fixer. That's not a channel. That's a pipeline."

Jordan doesn't deny it. They take another sip of the matcha, and the steam fogs their glasses for a moment. When they pull the frames off to clean them, I see their eyes — really see them — and there's something raw underneath the polish. Something fraying.

"You don't understand the architecture," Jordan says quietly. "Tom has his hands in everything. If I'm not useful to him, I'm a liability. And liabilities in Tom's world don't get to stay in the room."
"And Jax?"
"Jax can take care of herself." Jordan says it with something that might be admiration, might be resentment. "She always could."

I study them — the slight tremor in the hand, the way they won't hold my gaze for more than three seconds, the matcha latte going cold between us. Jordan Keeler is playing both sides, and the board is getting crowded, and I can see it in their body even if their mouth won't say it: they don't know how to get off without getting crushed.
"Maya." Jordan reaches out, touches my arm. Their fingers are cold through my sleeve. "Don't make this bigger than it is."
"I'm a documentarian," I say. "I don't make things big. I just record what's already there."
Jordan's mouth quirks — not quite a smile. "That's the same thing."
They walk away, and I let them go. The matcha steam has stopped. Their hand is still shaking.
I tell Jax that same night.

Cliff House is quiet — that particular quiet of a space that's too expensive to feel warm. The fire is going in the main room, and Jax is standing at the window with a glass of something amber, looking out at the Long Island Sound like she's trying to read the future in the dark water.
I show her the photo on my camera. The manila envelope. The label. Whitfield's silver head bent toward Jordan's ear.
And Jax Vance does something I don't expect.
Nothing.
She doesn't flinch. Doesn't swear. Doesn't set down her glass. She looks at the image for exactly three seconds, and then she turns back to the window and says, voice calm as still water: "I know."
"You — what?"

"I've known Jordan reports to Tom for two years." Jax takes a sip. The amber liquid catches the firelight. "I've been feeding Jordan curated information for exactly that long. Half-truths that make me look reckless and keep Tom comfortable. Little things. A missed board meeting. A number that's slightly wrong. A quote about expansion that I'd never actually say in a room that matters."
The room is very still. The fire pops. A log shifts.
I stare at her. "You've been running a counter-intelligence operation on your own friend?"
Jax turns from the window, and her face is — I don't know. Controlled. The way a dam is controlled: enormous pressure held back by structure and will.
"Jordan isn't my friend." Each word is distinct, separate, like she's placing stones. "Jordan is a channel."

The word hits me harder than it should. Jordan used the same one, hours ago, standing on a cold sidewalk with a matcha latte. *I'm a channel.* And now Jax, standing in a house that cost more than a city block, saying the same thing from the other side.
"Jax." My voice comes out quieter than I want. "You've been lying to someone who trusts you. For two years. That's not strategy — that's —"

"Survival." Her jaw tightens. "Tom is building something, Maya. I don't know what yet, but I know it ends with me losing everything unless I stay three moves ahead. And I can't stay ahead if I'm sentimental about who's passing information."
"Even if the person passing it thinks they're your friend?"

The silence that follows is the longest I've ever heard from Jax Vance. The fire crackles. The Sound moves against the rocks below. And something shifts in Jax's expression — a hairline fracture, invisible unless you're standing exactly where I'm standing.
"It costs me," she says finally. "You think it doesn't cost me?"

She sets down her glass. She doesn't look at me when she says it, and that — Jax, who meets every gaze head-on, who treats eye contact like a fencing match — that's how I know it's true. That's how I know the calculation isn't cold. It's the opposite of cold. It's something so hot she can barely hold it, so she pretends it's ice.
I leave it there. I don't have the right words, and the wrong ones would shatter something.
The call comes at 1:47 AM.

My phone screen is cold against my ear — that particular winter-midnight cold that makes you feel like the device is drawing heat from your skull. I fumble for it in the dark of my rented room, the glow hurting my eyes, and when I answer, Jordan's voice is wrong.
Not arch. Not flirtatious. Not the Jordan Keeler who weaponizes charm like a scalpel.
Scared.

"Whitfield asked me to plant a device." The words come fast, low, like they're afraid the walls are already listening. "In Cliff House. Something that tracks data — network traffic, emails, documents. He gave me the hardware today. It's small, Maya. Smaller than a book. I'm supposed to put it in Jax's office during the charity lunch on Thursday when the house is open to the caterers."
I sit up in bed. The sheets are cold against my back.
"Tom isn't just gathering intel anymore," Jordan continues. "He's building a case. Whitfield used that word — *case.* Like legal case. Like something that goes to court."
"Jordan—"

"If I refuse, Tom cuts me off. And I don't mean he stops returning my texts. I mean I stop existing in any room that matters. My whole life — every connection, every relationship — it runs through his infrastructure. I'm the wire, Maya. You don't negotiate with the wire. You replace it."

Their breathing is jagged. I can hear it through the phone, the way it catches and stutters, and I picture them somewhere — their apartment, probably, that minimalist place in Chelsea with the white walls and the art they can't really afford — pacing, alone, the cold phone pressed to their face.
"And if you comply?" I ask, though I already know.

"Then I betray Jax. Really betray her. Not gossip — surveillance. Not whispers in a dining room — hard data that Whitfield can use to build a legal case against her. That's different. That's — I didn't sign up for this, Maya. I signed up for knowing things. For being useful. Not for this."
I close my eyes. The phone is so cold it feels like it's burning my skin.
"I'll talk to Jax," I say. "Don't do anything yet. Give me until tomorrow night."
Jordan is silent for a long moment. When they speak again, their voice is barely a whisper: "Tomorrow night might be too late."
I go to Cliff House at dawn.

The light is grey and thin, the kind of Long Island morning that makes everything look like it was shot on expired film. Jax is already up — of course she is — in the kitchen, standing at the counter with black coffee and her tablet, scanning something that makes her forehead crease.
I tell her everything. The device. The charity lunch. Whitfield building a case. The terror in Jordan's voice.
And Jax — Jax just nods.
"Tell Jordan to plant it."
I must mishear. "What?"

"Tell them to plant the device." Jax sets down her tablet. Her eyes are clear, sharp, focused in a way that makes the hair on my arms stand up. "I'll control what it sees. I've already segmented my network — have for two years. There's a partition that looks like my real system but isn't. It's where all the 'leaks' Jordan has ever passed along actually come from. I don't store anything sensitive on the main network. Never have. The device will see exactly what I want Tom to see."
A double agent within a double agent. A Russian nesting doll of betrayals, each one nested inside the last.

"Jax." My voice is steady, but something underneath it isn't. "You're asking Jordan to walk into your house and install surveillance hardware. You're asking me to be the messenger. You're running an operation on people who think they're your friends — or at least your allies — and you're telling me this is strategy."

"It *is* strategy." Jax picks up her coffee. She takes a sip. She's absolutely still, and that stillness is the scariest thing I've ever seen from her. "Tom is building a legal case against me. Real or fabricated, it doesn't matter — once it exists, it has weight. The only way to neutralize it is to feed it false evidence from the inside. Make the case look thin. Make me look careless but not criminal. Keep Tom comfortable and overconfident." She pauses. "Give Tom exactly what I want him to see."

It's brilliant. I can see that. It's the kind of play that only works if you're three steps ahead and willing to sacrifice every piece on the board — including the ones that think they're playing their own game. Jax isn't just countering Tom's move. She's turning his entire strategy into an extension of hers.
But the cost.

I see it in the way she won't quite look at me when she says *Jordan isn't my friend.* I see it in the architecture of her face, the way she's built walls so high and so smooth that nothing can climb them — including affection, including warmth, including the basic human need to be known by someone who isn't calculating your utility.

Jax Vance is treating every relationship as a transaction. Channel, asset, pipeline, wire. And maybe she has to. Maybe that's the price of surviving when your father is building a cage around you and calling it protection.

But Jordan is terrified. And I am the messenger. And somewhere in the calculus of Jax's strategy, the actual people — the ones with trembling hands and cold matcha lattes and ragged breathing at 1:47 AM — are becoming variables.
The device is going in tomorrow.
That night, I sit at the small desk in my room with my notebook open. The pen moves before I can stop it.
*Everyone in this story is using everyone.*

*Jax uses Jordan — channels, half-truths, controlled leaks. Tom uses Jordan — envelopes, fixers, legal cases. Jordan uses both of them — access, proximity, the fragile scaffolding of social survival.*
*And I'm using all of them for my documentary.*

*I tell myself it's different. I tell myself I'm bearing witness, not exploiting. But I raised my camera in that hallway at Soho House the way a soldier raises a rifle — instinct, training, and no time to ask whether the shot was ethical. The photo exists now. The information exists. And the moment I chose to take it, I chose a side, even if I haven't admitted which one.*
*When does the using stop and the person begin?*
I stare at the words. They don't answer me.

Somewhere across the city, Jordan Keeler is lying awake with a device in their possession that could end everything. Somewhere at Cliff House, Jax Vance is calibrating a false network with the precision of a watchmaker. Somewhere in a glass tower, Tom Hargrove and his fixer are building a case, convinced they're pulling strings they can see.

The device goes in tomorrow. And nobody — not even Jax, with her partitions and her projections and her two-year chess game — can control every variable. One flicker in the network. One moment of genuine data slipping through. One person making a choice that doesn't align with the strategy.
I close the notebook. The dark is absolute.

Tomorrow, someone is going to plant something in Cliff House, and the walls are going to have eyes, and the only thing standing between Jax Vance and destruction is a former socialite who's scared and a filmmaker who's beginning to suspect she's part of the story she's trying to tell.
I don't sleep.
